summary: The data industry is experiencing a dual shock: massive consolidation of established players (exemplified by the Fivetran-dbt merger) and a stark reality check for AI initiatives, with 95% of enterprise AI pilots reportedly failing to deliver ROI. While practitioners debate the future of open-source dbt and vendor lock-in concerns, the conversation around AI has matured from hype to hard questions about costs, compliance, and actual utility.
key_points: Fivetran-dbt merger signals end of MDS 1.0 era and validates bundling over best-of-breed approaches
Practitioner community skeptical about long-term commitment to dbt Core open-source
95% of enterprise AI pilots failing to deliver measurable ROI according to MIT study
Hidden AI costs extend beyond API fees to cloud expenses, data maintenance, and attribution complexity
Career anxiety is unprecedented, with juniors struggling to find entry-level roles and seniors needing to transition to AI-first skills
money_quotes: The data industry is facing a dual shock: a massive consolidation of the old guard and a stark reality check for the new.
The 2023-2024 'chat-with-your-data' euphoria has slammed into the hard realities of 2025, where chatting with data is a nice-to-have, but not a necessity.
It's no longer enough to be a 'data plumber' who just knows SQL and Python.
The people who can transition their careers to become AI-first are in the driver's seat for shaping the future. Those who wait this out will likely be left very far behind.
There's a level of fear about the future I haven't seen, even during the Great Financial Crisis.
key_insight: The modern data stack era is ending through consolidation while AI faces a utility reckoning, and professionals who fail to adapt to an AI-first approach risk being left behind regardless of seniority level.
